answer:
In Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, chapter 15 ("Of physical pains and pleasures considered as elements of the power of laws. — Of the judgments that have been passed on pleasures and pains of this kind, by religious sects and by philosophical sects"), Comte argues that Stoic "contempt for physical pains and pleasures" arose sociologically amid the insecurity of ancient Greece and Rome—specifically, amid proscriptions (e.g., Marius, Sulla, triumvirs), confiscations, enslavement risks, and tyrannical reigns like those of Tiberius and Nero—rather than as metaphysical doctrine.[^1] Such maxims, he notes, prepared men to expect "exile, confiscation, the loss of one’s family, and proscription, as one foresees the simplest events in the ordinary course of life," rendering them equally applicable to "a slave of our modern colonists" as to "a subject of Nero."[^2]

The precise line drawing the slave comparison is: "The maxims of Epictetus would suit a slave of our modern colonists no less than a subject of Nero."[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 15](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/15-des-peines-et-des-plaisirs-physiques-consideres-co/translation/#p-73b670).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 15](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/15-des-peines-et-des-plaisirs-physiques-consideres-co/translation/#p-73b670).
[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/15-des-peines-et-des-plaisirs-physiques-consideres-co/translation/#p-73b670).
